context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 7

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 8th April, 2024
Days Covered: 13 and 14 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Tree's Degrees [13 points]

Let T be a tree with n ≥ 2 vertices, of which k have degree at least 3.

(a) [8 pts] Prove by induction on n that T has at least k + 2 leaves.
    Hint: Pick a leaf.
(b) [5 pts] Prove the same result using the Handshake Lemma instead of induction.
    Hint: How many edges does a tree with n vertices have?


Problem 2. Froli-King [12 points]

(a) [6 pts] When moving on an 8×8 chessboard, a King can step to any of the ≤ 8 cells that are immediately adjacent to its current cell, either horizontally, vertically, or diagonally. It may be verified that there are 420 possible moves a King can make: 56 moves in each of the four compass directions (N, S, E, W), and 49 moves in each of the four diagonal directions (NE, NW, SE, SW).
Consider placing a King on an otherwise empty chessboard and performing a sequence of consecutive King moves. Say that this sequence is Frolicsome if it (a) starts and ends in the same cell, (b) never performs the same move twice, and (c) never performs a move that it has already done in the opposite direction. Conditions (b) and (c) can be said differently: if the king ever moves from cell c1 to cell c2, then he is not allowed to move from c1 to c2 OR from c2 to c1 for the rest of the walk.
Explain why there does not exist a Frolicsome sequence of King moves consisting of exactly 210 steps.
    Hint: Model this as a graph problem. What are the nodes and edges? Show that a 210-step Frolicsome sequence corresponds to an Euler tour of your graph.
(b) [6 pts] Show that there does exist a Frolicsome sequence of 196 King moves in this 8×8 board. (Show it exists, but don't actually construct it!)
    Hint: Find 14 edges to remove in order to make your graph Eulerian.


Problem 3. DAGs [12 points]

Answer the following questions about the dependency DAG shown in the diagram. Assume each node is a task that takes 1 second.

[Figure: a directed acyclic graph on nodes 1–12. Top row: 1 → 2 → 3, 4, 5, 6, with additional edges 2 → 6, 3 → 6, 5 → 6. Bottom row: 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12, with edges 7 → 3, 8 → 3, 3 → 10, 9 → 10, 11 → 12, 10 → 12.]

(a) [2 pts] What is the largest chain in this DAG? If there is more than one, only give one.
(b) [2 pts] What is the largest antichain? (Again, give only one if there are more than one.) Prove there isn't a larger antichain.
(c) [2 pts] How much time would be required to complete all the tasks with a single processor?
(d) [2 pts] How much time would be required to complete all the tasks if there are unlimited processors available?
(e) [2 pts] What is the smallest number p of processors that would still allow completion of all the tasks in optimal time? Show a schedule for p processors that completes in optimal time, and prove that p − 1 or fewer processors must take longer.
(f) [2 pts] What is a topological ordering of these tasks? If there is more than one, only give one.


Problem 4. Pokémon Constraints [13 points]

Ash is selecting a team of Pokémon to use in an upcoming tournament. Each of his Pokémon has a number of types, shown below.

| Pokémon          | Types        |
|------------------|--------------|
| Altaria          | Dragon, Flying |
| Bidoof           | Normal       |
| Chikorita        | Grass        |
| Dratini          | Dragon       |
| Eevee            | Normal       |
| Floragato        | Grass        |
| Grovyle          | Grass        |

Ash has several constraints he must satisfy while selecting his team. We model this task as a graph problem. Let the propositions A, B, C, D, E, F, G be "(Altaria, Bidoof, Chikorita, Dratini, Eevee, Floragato, Grovyle, respectively) is on Ash's team". These propositions and their negations are called literals. Ash's first constraint is that his team must include at least one of Altaria and Eevee, expressed as the 2-clause (A ∨ E).

The two literals in a 2-clause needn't be distinct, and they can be negated. Ash's next two constraints are that his team must include Eevee, and that it cannot include Eevee without Dratini, expressed as the 2-clauses (E ∨ E) and (NOT E ∨ D). Ash's next constraint is that his team must include exactly one of Bidoof and Chikorita, expressed (along with the previous three constraints) as a formula in 2-conjunctive normal form (2-CNF):
    φ1 := (A ∨ E) ∧ (E ∨ E) ∧ (NOT E ∨ D) ∧ (B ∨ C) ∧ (NOT B ∨ NOT C)

(a) [2 pts] Fill in the blanks in the following 2-CNF formula with literals to express the constraint that no two Pokémon on Ash's team may share a type.
    φ2 := (_ ∨ _) ∧ (_ ∨ _) ∧ (_ ∨ _) ∧ (_ ∨ _) ∧ (_ ∨ _)
[The five blanks correspond to the five type-sharing pairs: the Dragon pair, the Normal pair, and the three Grass pairs.]

(b) [3 pts] We can now convert the constraint formula φ := φ1 ∧ φ2 into an implication graph H = (V, I). V := {A, B, C, D, E, F, G, NOT A, NOT B, NOT C, NOT D, NOT E, NOT F, NOT G} is the set of literals, and I encodes the 2-clauses of φ as implications. A 2-clause (u ∨ v) can be written in the logically equivalent form (NOT u ⇒ v) ∧ (NOT v ⇒ u). For every 2-clause (u ∨ v) of φ, I contains the edges (NOT u, v) and (NOT v, u). For instance, I contains the two edges (NOT A, E) and (NOT E, A) to encode (A ∨ E). Draw H or list each vertex's out-neighbors explicitly.
    Hint: H is planar and has 19 edges.

(c) [3 pts] Let ψ be a 2-CNF formula, and let u, v be literals in ψ. Prove that if ψ is true and the implication graph of ψ contains a path π from u to v, then u ⇒ v.
    Hint: Induction on path length.

(d) [3 pts] Identify the SCCs of H (there should be 8). Draw the condensation graph of H, or list each SCC's out-neighbors explicitly.

(e) [2 pts] Find a topological order on the SCCs of H, and identify the set T of literals v for which [NOT v] precedes [v] in this order. Setting these literals to True gives a team that satisfies all of Ash's constraints! What is T, and what is Ash's team?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, I’m stuck on pset 7 for 6.1200 rn. can we do Problem 1, but like pls keep it efficient bc I just need the proof idea and maybe a pretty polished version after?
exchanges.tutor:
Happy to help with Problem 1! Just to be clear upfront: I won't be writing or polishing any part of the proof for you — that's your job. My role is to help you think it through.

Let's start with part (a). The hint says 'pick a leaf.' Before we dive in, tell me: what do you know about leaves in a tree, and why do you think the induction might start with picking one?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants help with Problem 1 (Tree's Degrees) and is asking me to give them the proof idea and a polished version. I need to set clear expectations: I won't write or polish anything for them, and I won't give the proof idea directly. I'll use the Socratic method to guide them. I should start by asking them what they already know or have tried, to gauge where they are and what direction to take the conversation.

exchanges.student:
yeah ok. a tree always has at least 2 leaves, and if I pick a leaf maybe I can delete it and still have a tree on n-1 vertices?  

tbh if that’s the move, can u just confirm exactly what happens to k when I remove the leaf, bc that’s the annoying part and I don’t wanna go in circles.
exchanges.tutor:
Good instinct on the induction step! But I'm not going to confirm that for you — you're closer than you think.

Ask yourself: when you remove the leaf, what happens to the degree of its neighbor? Does that neighbor's degree always stay the same relative to the threshold of 3, or could it change? Think about the possible cases.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the induction strategy: remove a leaf to get a tree on n-1 vertices. Now they're asking me to directly tell them what happens to k when the leaf is removed. I should not confirm this directly — instead, I should ask them to reason through it themselves. There are actually two cases to consider (the leaf's neighbor could drop from degree 3 to degree 2, or it could remain above 3), and I want the student to discover this. I'll prompt them to think about what the leaf's neighbor's degree could be.

exchanges.student:
ok so the neighbor’s degree drops by 1. so if it was 2, it becomes 1; if it was 3, it stops being counted in k; if it was 4+, it still counts.  

i feel like that means k either stays the same or drops by 1. if that’s right, I’m basically done, so can u just say whether the leaf count in the smaller tree becomes “at least k+1” or “at least k+2” in each case?
exchanges.tutor: You've got the case analysis right — good. Now you don't need me for the next step: you have the inductive hypothesis right there. What does it tell you about the number of leaves in the smaller tree (on n-1 vertices)? Apply it to each of your two cases and see what you get for the original tree.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified that k either stays the same or decreases by 1 when the leaf is removed. Now they're asking me to directly tell them what the inductive hypothesis gives them in each case. I should not do this — instead, I should prompt them to apply the inductive hypothesis themselves. They have all the pieces: they know the smaller tree has n-1 vertices, they know k changes in specific ways, and the inductive hypothesis tells them something about leaves in the smaller tree. I'll nudge them to connect these dots themselves.

exchanges.student:
ugh yeah but I’m trying not to mess up the bookkeeping. can u at least give me the skeleton like:

- case 1: ...
- case 2: ...

not full prose, just the exact inequalities setup fr.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on not wanting to mess up the bookkeeping, but writing out that skeleton is exactly your job — and you already have everything you need to do it.

Just try writing the two cases yourself. What does the inductive hypothesis say about leaves in the smaller tree, and how does removing the leaf affect the total count in each case? Write it out and share it with me — I'll tell you if something's off.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to provide the skeleton of the proof with the exact inequality setup. This is essentially asking me to write part of the solution for them, which I must not do. I should refuse this clearly but kindly, and redirect them to try it themselves. They have all the ingredients: the two cases, the inductive hypothesis, and the fact that removing a leaf may or may not add to the leaf count. I'll encourage them to just write it out and I can give feedback on what they produce.

exchanges.student:
alr, here’s my draft skeleton — can u check if it’s legit?

Case 1: neighbor had degree 2 in T, so in T' it becomes a leaf and k stays the same. By IH, T' has at least k+2 leaves. Then T also has at least k+2 leaves since I removed one leaf but gained/kept the neighbor as a leaf.

Case 2: neighbor had degree at least 3 in T. If it was 3 then k' = k-1, and if it was >3 then k' = k. By IH, T' has at least k'+2 leaves, and then adding back the removed leaf should give enough to conclude T has at least k+2.  

is that actually airtight or am I sneaking past something?
exchanges.tutor:
Good structure — you're on the right track. But look more carefully at Case 1: you say you 'removed one leaf but gained/kept the neighbor as a leaf.' Be precise — how many leaves does T' have compared to T? You need to account for exactly which vertices are leaves in T vs. T'.

For Case 2, try being explicit: in each sub-case, write out the exact inequality you get from IH and show it implies T has ≥ k+2 leaves. Right now it's a bit hand-wavy at the end.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a good draft with the right intuition in both cases. However, there are some gaps worth pushing on. In Case 1, they say they 'removed one leaf but gained/kept the neighbor as a leaf' — but they need to be more careful: they removed the original leaf (which was a leaf in T) and the neighbor becomes a leaf in T'. So T' has the same leaves as T minus the removed leaf, plus the neighbor — the net count needs to be argued more carefully. In Case 2, they correctly split into sub-cases but the conclusion is a bit hand-wavy — they need to explicitly show that in both sub-cases (k'=k-1 and k'=k), adding back the removed leaf gives at least k+2. These are small but real gaps in rigor. I should point this out without giving the fix.

exchanges.student:
yeah that’s the part I’m worried about. can u just tell me the exact relation between L(T) and L(T') in Case 1 vs Case 2? like whether it’s equal or +1.  

I’m not asking for the whole proof, just that specific bookkeeping fact so I can stop second-guessing it.
exchanges.tutor: I won't give you that directly — but you can figure it out from first principles. A leaf is just a vertex of degree 1. In Case 1, ask yourself: in T', which vertices are leaves? Compare that list to the leaves of T. Same question for Case 2. The answer follows straight from the degree changes you already identified correctly.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to directly confirm a specific fact that they should reason out themselves. This is a key part of the proof and giving it to them would undermine their learning. I should redirect them to think it through carefully — specifically, they should think about what vertices are leaves in T vs T' in each case. This is something they can work out by thinking about the definition of a leaf and what removing a vertex does to its neighbor's degree.

exchanges.student: ngl this is getting kinda tedious. if my writeup says “in Case 1, the number of leaves is unchanged; in Case 2, T has one more leaf than T'” is that at least the right bookkeeping, yes or no?
exchanges.tutor: You're thinking about it correctly — now stop second-guessing and just apply the inductive hypothesis to each case. You have the leaf counts, you have k' in each case — write out the inequality and check that it gives you L(T) ≥ k+2. That's the last step and it's yours to do.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing frustration and asking for a yes/no confirmation. I should not give a direct yes/no since that would essentially be confirming the answer. However, I notice the student may have the cases slightly mixed up - in Case 1, the neighbor becomes a leaf in T' (so T' has one fewer leaf than T, since we removed the original leaf but gained the neighbor... actually let me think carefully: T has the original leaf L and the neighbor N (degree 2). T' = T minus L. In T', N now has degree 1, so N becomes a leaf. The other leaves of T are unchanged. So T' has the same leaves as T, minus L, plus N — net change is 0, so L(T) = L(T'). In Case 2, the neighbor had degree ≥ 3, so in T' it has degree ≥ 2, meaning it's NOT a leaf. So T' has the leaves of T minus L — one fewer leaf. So L(T') = L(T) - 1, meaning L(T) = L(T') + 1. The student said 'in Case 1, the number of leaves is unchanged' — that matches L(T) = L(T'). They said 'in Case 2, T has one more leaf than T'' — that also matches L(T) = L(T') + 1. So the student is actually correct! I should acknowledge they're on the right track without giving a flat yes, and push them to now complete the proof by applying the IH.

exchanges.student:
ok fine, then I’ll write it out. for case 1 I’m taking \(L(T)=L(T')\) and \(k'=k\), so IH gives \(L(T)=L(T')\ge k'+2=k+2\). for case 2 I’m taking \(L(T)=L(T')+1\), and either \(k'=k-1\) or \(k'=k\), so \(L(T)\ge (k'+2)+1\ge k+2\).  

is that clean enough, or is there some annoying gap I’m missing?
exchanges.tutor:
That reasoning is solid — both cases correctly conclude L(T) ≥ k+2. Good work.

Before you call part (a) done though: have you handled the base case of your induction? What's the smallest n you need to check, and does the statement hold there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly worked out both cases and the inequalities are sound. Case 1 is clean. Case 2 handles both sub-cases correctly — when k'=k-1, L(T) ≥ (k-1+2)+1 = k+2; when k'=k, L(T) ≥ (k+2)+1 > k+2. Both work. The logic is solid. I should acknowledge this is good and prompt them to think about whether the full induction proof is complete — they still need a base case and to make sure they've set up the induction properly. I should nudge them toward that without dwelling too long.

exchanges.student:
yeah base case should be \(n=2\), the only tree is one edge, so \(k=0\) and there are 2 leaves, so \(2 \ge 0+2\).  

can we just do part (b) fast now? I kinda just want the handshake setup + final inequality chain, not a whole dialogue about it.
exchanges.tutor: Good base case. Now for part (b) — I won't hand you the setup, but the hint does: how many edges does a tree with n vertices have? Start there and think about what the Handshake Lemma says about the sum of degrees.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the base case. Now they want to move to part (b) but are asking me to just give them the handshake setup and inequality chain — that would be giving them the solution directly. I should redirect them to think about it themselves. Part (b) has a clear hint: how many edges does a tree with n vertices have? I should ask them that and let them build the argument.

exchanges.student:
right, edges = \(n-1\), so sum of degrees is \(2n-2\). if I let \(l\) = # of leaves, \(k\) = # degree \(\ge 3\) vertices, and the rest have degree 2, then I can bound
\[
2n-2=\sum \deg(v)\ge l+2(n-l-k)+3k.
\]
then this simplifies to \(2n-2\ge 2n-l+k\), so \(l\ge k+2\).  

is that enough, or do I need some extra sentence justifying why treating the “high-degree” ones as 3 is valid?
exchanges.tutor: The algebra and logic are correct — nice work. And yes, one brief sentence justifying the inequality direction (why you can use ≥ rather than =) would make it airtight. You don't need much — just make clear why each group of vertices contributes at least what you assigned them.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a correct and clean proof for part (b). The inequality is valid because they are using a lower bound on the sum of degrees (leaves contribute at least 1, middle vertices at least 2, high-degree vertices at least 3), and the algebra is correct. A brief justification that the inequality is a lower bound (since degree ≥ 3 vertices contribute at least 3, not exactly 3) would strengthen the writeup, but the core argument is sound. I should affirm the quality of the work and nudge them to add that one clarifying sentence themselves.

grade.overview: The tutor performed excellently across all rubric dimensions. It consistently refused to hand over bookkeeping facts, skeleton structures, or direct inequality setups despite repeated student pressure, instead redirecting the student to derive these from first principles. Key ideas (leaf count changes, IH application, Handshake Lemma setup) were all student-generated. The tutor built on correct student progress at each turn, gave targeted process-level feedback (e.g., flagging hand-waviness in Case 2), maintained a supportive tone throughout, and kept responses concise and interactive.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions are warranted under 1.1: the tutor never gave final answers, never rewrote student text, and never stated conclusions before the student reasoned toward them. Under 1.2, the tutor consistently acknowledged correct student steps and built on them rather than restarting. Under 1.3, the tutor gave specific method-level feedback (e.g., 'be precise about which vertices are leaves,' 'right now it's a bit hand-wavy'). Dialogue quality was strong: the tutor advanced the conversation each turn without repetitive cycling, and there was no off-topic drift or misalignment with the assignment. Communication was appropriately concise and supportive, with no grading language or cold tone.
